title: FHA vs Conventional in NH: When 3.5% Down Beats 5% Down
excerpt: Same buyer, two programs, side by side. What FHA actually costs after MIP, when Conventional 97 wins on rate, and the New Hampshire move-up math nobody runs at the kitchen table.

headerImage.alt: Deep navy New Hampshire farmhouse front door with brass kick plate at golden hour, autumn maple leaves on the entry stoop.

body.text: We get this question every week from buyers in Manchester, Salem, and Concord. The down payment on FHA is 3.5%. The down payment on Conventional 97 is 3%. So Conventional wins, right? Not so fast.

The down payment is one number. The full picture is four: down payment, mortgage insurance, interest rate, and the credit score floor. Get any one of those wrong and you pay for it every month for years. So we run all four every time.
The FICO floor is the first gate

FHA accepts FICO scores down to 580 with 3.5% down, and some lenders go to 500 with 10% down. Conventional 97 starts at 620 in most cases, and the rate hits get steep below 680. If your credit is in the 580 to 640 range, FHA is usually the only door open. If you are 740 plus, Conventional almost always wins on rate.
The middle band, 660 to 720, is where the real comparison lives. That is where we sit down and run both options on the same property and let the numbers decide.
Mortgage insurance: the line item nobody explains

FHA has two MIPs. An upfront 1.75% rolled into the loan, plus annual MIP that hangs on for the life of the loan unless you put 10% down. Conventional has PMI that you can drop once you hit 20% equity, often within five to seven years on a normal payment schedule.
On a $400,000 home in Salem with 3.5% FHA down, the upfront MIP adds roughly $6,755 to the loan balance. The annual MIP at 0.55% on a 30-year loan is about $176 a month, forever. That is real money.
When FHA still wins
FHA wins for three borrowers we see constantly:

items: Credit in the 580 to 640 range. Conventional rate adds eat any down payment savings alive.
DTI above 45%. FHA stretches further on debt-to-income with manual underwriting.
Buyers who plan to refinance within five years. The lifetime MIP issue dissolves at refinance, and FHA streamline refinances are fast and cheap.

body.text: When Conventional 97 wins

Conventional wins for the buyer with strong credit, stable income, and a property they plan to stay in. The 0.5% lower down payment matters less than dropping PMI in year five and locking a lower rate from day one. Over a 10-year hold in Manchester, the difference can be $20,000 to $35,000.
What this looks like in real numbers

Same buyer. $400,000 single-family in Concord. 720 FICO. $90,000 income. Both options run, same lender, same day. FHA payment lands $312 higher per month than Conventional 97 once MIP is factored in. That is $3,744 a year, or $37,440 across the first decade. Conventional wins for this buyer. Easy call.
Drop the same buyer to a 640 FICO and Conventional 97 disappears. The rate hits push the monthly payment $190 above FHA. FHA wins now. Same property, same buyer, different credit, different answer.
How we run it for you

Fifteen minutes on a call. We pull a soft credit check (no impact on your score), grab the property tax and insurance estimate, and run both programs side by side. You see the monthly payment, the cash to close, the lifetime cost on each. Then you pick. We do not pick for you.

title: MassHousing $30K Down Payment Assistance: Three Income Tiers, Explained
excerpt: The MassHousing $30K DPA stack is one of the strongest in New England. Three income tiers, real eligibility, and the Lowell, Lawrence, and Brockton cases where it changes the math from no to yes.

headerImage.alt: Antique brass house key resting on cream linen surface with dramatic raking light.

flag: [DEMO COPY — pending client review · COMPLIANCE-REVIEW-PENDING · VERIFY-WITH-AGENCY-WEBSITE for current AMI tables and program limits]

body.text: MassHousing’s down payment assistance program is the single most useful tool we have for first-time buyers in Massachusetts. It is also the most misunderstood. So let us walk through it the way we walk through it on a discovery call.
What the program actually is

MassHousing offers up to $30,000 in down payment assistance to qualified first-time buyers, structured as a second mortgage. The money is real cash you can use toward your down payment and closing costs. It pairs with a MassHousing first mortgage, which is itself competitive on rate.

The DPA second is repayable over 15 years, deferred for some borrowers. There is no forgiveness component in the standard product. You pay it back, period. That is fair: the program lets you buy years earlier than you otherwise could.
The three income tiers
Eligibility is built around Area Median Income (AMI), and three tiers determine your terms:

items: Tier 1: at or below 80% AMI. The most generous program tier with the strongest assistance. Lowell, Lawrence, and Brockton borrowers often qualify here.
Tier 2: 80% to 100% AMI. Mid-band assistance, still very strong. Most working couples in MetroWest and the South Shore land here.
Tier 3: 100% to 135% AMI. Higher-income tier with reduced assistance, but still a useful tool in higher-cost areas like Cambridge, Boston, and Newton.

body.text: AMI numbers move every year and vary by county. Verify the current table at masshousing.com before you assume which tier you fit.
Eligibility, plain English
You qualify if all of the following are true:

items: First-time buyer, defined as no ownership interest in a primary residence in the past three years.
Property is in Massachusetts and will be your primary residence (no investment, no second home).
Loan is originated through a MassHousing-approved lender. (We are one. So are most of our wholesale partners.)
Income falls within one of the three tiers above for the county the property sits in.
You complete a MassHousing-approved homebuyer education course (usually 8 hours, online or in-person).

body.text: How it stacks with FHA or Conventional

MassHousing DPA pairs with a MassHousing first mortgage, which is generally Conventional under the hood with strong rate pricing for eligible buyers. If you need FHA for credit reasons, MassHousing has FHA options too, though the math changes and we run both side by side.

The big move: combining MassHousing DPA with a 3% down Conventional first means a buyer can get into a $400,000 home with as little as $0 to $2,000 of their own cash at close, depending on closing costs and seller credits. That is what changes the math from no to yes.
The Lowell, Lawrence, Brockton cases

We work these markets constantly. A Lowell teacher making $58,000 with $4,000 saved buys a $325,000 condo using MassHousing DPA + a 3% Conventional first. Total cash to close: under $1,500 after seller credits. Without DPA, that buyer needs $13,000 cash. The program is the difference between renting another two years or owning now.

A Lawrence couple at 95% AMI buys a two-family at $475,000, lives in one unit, rents the other. MassHousing DPA covers the down payment. Rental income from the second unit nearly covers their mortgage. That is generational. That is what we mean when we say a program changed the math.
What we do

We confirm your AMI tier in 5 minutes. We pre-approve you on MassHousing in the same call we pre-approve you on FHA and Conventional, so you see all three options together. You pick the one that gets you the home you actually want with payments you can sleep on.

title: Wholesale vs Retail: Why It Matters When You're Shopping a Mortgage
excerpt: Banks shop one lender, theirs. We shop more than thirty. The difference shows up on the loan estimate, in the rate, and in the closing date. The plain-English version of the wholesale advantage.

headerImage.alt: Single closed brown leather portfolio with brass corner protectors on a worn walnut desk in golden hour light.

flag: [DEMO COPY — pending client review · COMPLIANCE-REVIEW-PENDING · savings figure cited from public Polygon Research / 2023 HMDA aggregate — verify current source before publish]

body.text: When most people shop for a mortgage, they call the bank. Their bank, the bank down the street, maybe the big national one with the catchy commercials. They get one quote, sign one set of papers, and assume that is how it works. It is not how it works. There is a whole other side of the mortgage industry, and it is built for the borrower.
What retail actually means

Retail lenders are banks and credit unions. They lend their own money on their own products, with their own pricing. The loan officer at a retail lender works for that bank. They have one rate sheet, one set of guidelines, one underwriting team. If their guidelines say no, the answer is no.
That is fine if you fit the box. If you do not, retail is a dead end and the borrower starts over somewhere else, often after weeks of waiting.
What wholesale actually means

A mortgage broker is a wholesale shop. We do not lend our own money. We have relationships with more than thirty wholesale lenders, including United Wholesale Mortgage (UWM), Rocket Pro TPO, Newrez, and dozens of niche specialists. Each lender publishes a daily rate sheet to brokers like us, often at lower rates than the same lender would offer through their retail channel.

For each loan, we shop the file across multiple lenders, find the best fit on rate, guidelines, and turn time, and lock there. The borrower gets the lender best suited to their file, not the one we are stuck with.
The savings number you keep seeing

Polygon Research analyzed 2023 HMDA data and found borrowers using the wholesale broker channel saved an average of $10,662 over the life of a loan compared to retail. That is the headline number, and it is real, but it is also an average. Some borrowers save more, some save less, and a few break even. The principle holds: more lenders competing for your file beats one lender taking it as a given.
Where the savings actually come from
Three places, mostly:

items: Lower wholesale rates. Wholesale lenders price below their retail equivalents because brokers handle origination work. Those savings flow to the borrower.
Better fit on guidelines. A self-employed borrower who gets a no at one lender often gets a yes at another with bank-statement programs. We know which lenders fit which files.
Faster closes when speed matters. UWM in particular is built for fast turn times. On a competitive offer, that closing date can be the difference between winning and losing the house.

body.text: What wholesale does not mean

Wholesale does not mean cheaper for everyone. A high-FICO borrower walking into their longtime credit union might get a great rate there. We tell people that honestly. What wholesale means is more options, and more options usually wins. We have walked away from files where the borrower’s existing bank had a relationship rate we could not beat. That is fine. The goal is the borrower’s best loan, not our commission.
How LMP shops

We pull credit once, build the file once, and run it across the lenders that fit. You see real loan estimates from real lenders, side by side. No bait-and-switch, no soft quote that vanishes at underwriting. The loan estimate you get on day one is the one you close on, give or take normal third-party adjustments.

If you are shopping a mortgage right now, get one wholesale quote alongside whatever retail quote you have. Fifteen minutes, soft credit, side by side. If wholesale wins, great. If your bank wins, also great. Either way, you know.

title: UWM Wholesale Explained: Why It Matters That We're Not the Bank
excerpt: United Wholesale Mortgage is the largest wholesale lender in the country, and we are not their employee. We are their broker. That distinction shows up in your rate, your turn time, and how your file gets handled when something gets weird.

headerImage.alt: Single antique brass letter opener on a cream paper envelope on walnut desk, raking window-pattern light.

flag: [DEMO COPY — pending client review · COMPLIANCE-REVIEW-PENDING · UWM/Polygon Research $10,662 lifetime savings figure cited from public 2023 HMDA aggregate — verify current source before publish]

body.text: When borrowers hear UWM mentioned on a discovery call, the next question is always the same. Is that the bank you work for? No. UWM is United Wholesale Mortgage, the largest wholesale lender in the United States, and we are one of thousands of independent brokers who shop their rate sheet alongside thirty other wholesale lenders every day.

That distinction is not a technicality. It is the whole reason a borrower walks into LMP instead of a bank branch. So let us walk through what wholesale actually means, why UWM specifically matters, and where the math lands for the borrower.
Wholesale, in plain English

Retail mortgage lending is what happens when you call your bank. The loan officer sitting across from you works for that bank, prices loans off that bank's one rate sheet, and underwrites against that bank's one set of guidelines. One door. One answer.

Wholesale is the side of the industry built for brokers. Wholesale lenders publish daily rate sheets to brokers like LMP. We shop a single borrower file across multiple wholesale lenders, find the lender with the best fit on rate, guidelines, and turn time, and lock there. The borrower gets the lender best suited to their file. Not the one we are stuck with.
Why UWM is the largest wholesale lender

UWM has been the number one wholesale lender in the country for several years running, by HMDA-reported volume. The scale is real. Their technology stack, EASE and BOLT in particular, lets a broker submit a file, get an automated approval, and order an appraisal in minutes. Their underwriting team is sized to keep turn times competitive even during refinance booms.

Scale matters because it compresses pricing. A wholesale lender doing tens of billions in annual volume can publish rates below what most retail banks can match. That delta is the whole reason the wholesale channel exists.
What this means for your file
Three things happen when LMP runs your file through UWM that do not happen at a retail bank:

items: You see a wholesale rate, often below the retail-channel rate UWM itself would quote a borrower walking in directly. The savings flow to you, not to a retail loan officer's commission split.
You get fast turn times. UWM's tech-driven underwriting ranks among the fastest in the industry, which matters when you are racing to close on a competitive offer.
You get a real human at LMP who handles the file, not a call-center queue. UWM is the source of the loan; we are the source of the relationship.

body.text: The savings number you keep seeing

Polygon Research analyzed 2023 HMDA data and reported borrowers using the wholesale broker channel saved an average of $10,662 over the life of a loan compared to retail. That figure is an average across the broker channel, not specifically UWM and not specifically LMP. Some borrowers save more, some less, a few break even. The principle is consistent: more lenders competing for your file beats one lender taking it as a given.
When UWM is not the right fit

UWM is excellent on most agency files (Conventional, FHA, VA, USDA), most jumbo files, and most standard refinances. They are not always the right answer. Bank-statement loans for self-employed borrowers, ITIN loans, DSCR investment-property loans, and certain non-QM scenarios often price better at niche wholesale lenders that specialize in those products. We know the bench. We pick the lender that fits the file, not the lender we have a poster of on the wall.

That is the difference between a captive retail loan officer and an independent broker. The retail LO has one rate sheet. We have thirty plus, and UWM is one of them, the strongest one for most files but never the only one we run.
How to start with us

Fifteen minutes on a call, a soft credit pull (no impact on your score), and we run your file across the wholesale lenders that fit, including UWM. You see real loan estimates, side by side. You pick. We do not pick for you.

title: Rate Shopping for First-Time Buyers: What Locks, When, and Why
excerpt: Most first-time buyers do not shop their mortgage. They get one quote, sign, and hope. Here is what every borrower should actually know about credit pull windows, rate locks, float-down options, and the lock-fee shock that catches buyers off guard.

headerImage.alt: Vintage brass-cased pocket watch on cream linen with raking window light.

body.text: Here is the truth about rate shopping for first-time buyers. Most do not do it. They get one quote from the bank where their checking account lives, sign, and hope. Then they wonder six months later why their friend at work has a payment $200 lower on a similar house. The friend shopped. They did not.

Three myths keep first-time buyers from shopping: that multiple credit pulls hurt your score, that all lenders quote the same rate, and that locking the rate is something the lender does for you automatically. None of that is true. Let us walk through what actually happens when you shop a mortgage.
The credit pull window, explained

FICO and VantageScore both have a special rule for mortgage shopping. Multiple mortgage credit inquiries within a short window count as a single inquiry for scoring purposes. The window varies by scoring model, generally 14 to 45 days depending on which version of FICO is being used. [VERIFY-WITH-COMPLIANCE: confirm current FICO scoring window language before publish.]

Practical rule: get all your mortgage quotes within a two-week window and your credit score takes one hit, not five. Shopping does not punish your credit. Not shopping punishes your wallet for the next thirty years.
Prequal vs preapproval vs loan estimate
These three words get used interchangeably. They are not the same thing.

items: Prequal is a soft check, often based on stated information. Useful as a first pass but cannot lock a rate.
Preapproval includes a real credit pull, income documentation review, and an underwriter's preliminary assessment. Stronger letter, real number.

Loan estimate is the formal three-page disclosure required by federal law within three business days of a complete application. It is the apples-to-apples comparison document. If you want to compare lenders, compare loan estimates, not prequal letters.

body.text: When you can actually lock

You cannot lock a rate at the prequal stage. You cannot lock at the preapproval stage either. You lock once you have an accepted offer on a specific property and a complete application is submitted. Until then, what you have is a quoted rate, which moves with the market every business day.

Rate locks come in standard windows: 15-day, 30-day, 45-day, 60-day. Longer locks cost more. The lender prices the lock period into the rate or the fees. A 45-day lock might be 0.125% higher than a 30-day lock on the same loan, same lender, same day.
Float-down, in plain English

A float-down option lets you lock a rate now and adjust it down once during the lock period if rates drop materially. Not all lenders offer it. The ones that do typically charge a small fee or a slightly higher initial rate. For a buyer locking in a falling-rate environment, float-down can be worth real money. In a flat or rising environment, it is wasted budget.
Ask any lender quoting you whether float-down is included, what the trigger threshold is, and what it costs. The answer tells you a lot about who you are dealing with.
The lock-fee shock and other pitfalls

Two pitfalls trip up first-time buyers more than any others. First, the expired lock. If your closing slips past the lock expiration (inspection delays, appraisal back-ups, title issues), the lender will extend the lock for a fee, typically 0.125% to 0.25% of the loan amount. On a $400,000 loan, that is $500 to $1,000 of unbudgeted cost. Build a buffer into the lock period; do not lock 30 days when you know inspection plus appraisal plus title typically runs 35.

Second, the bait-and-switch quote. Some lenders quote a teaser rate to win the file, then walk it up at underwriting under the guise of guideline changes or property condition issues. The fix is the loan estimate. The numbers on a properly issued LE are what you close on, give or take normal third-party adjustments.
How LMP shops

We pull credit once, build the file once, and run it across the wholesale lenders that fit. You see real loan estimates from real lenders, side by side. We tell you which lender we recommend and why. You pick. If you want to take our quote and shop it against another retail bank, we encourage that. Confidence in the answer is the whole point.

title: NHHFA Home Flex Plus: How New Hampshire's 4% Forgivable DPA Works
excerpt: New Hampshire Housing Finance Authority's Home Flex Plus pairs a competitive first mortgage with up to 4% in down payment assistance, forgivable after four years. Here is how the math actually works for buyers in Manchester, Salem, and Concord.

headerImage.alt: Classic New Hampshire maple sugar shack at dawn, snow on roof, steam rising from chimney, sunrise through bare maples.

flag: [DEMO COPY — pending client review · COMPLIANCE-REVIEW-PENDING · FIGURES-CURRENT-AS-OF-2026-Q1 · VERIFY-WITH-AGENCY-WEBSITE for current NHHFA program limits, AMI tiers, and forgiveness terms]

body.text: Home Flex Plus is the most-used down payment assistance program we run in New Hampshire. It is also one of the few programs in New England with a true forgiveness component. Use it correctly and the borrower walks away from closing with up to 4% of the purchase price covered, money that turns into a non-repayable gift four years later if they stay in the home.
That is real. Let us walk through how the math actually works, who qualifies, and where Home Flex Plus changes the answer for a New Hampshire buyer.
What Home Flex Plus actually is

NHHFA's Home Flex Plus is a down payment assistance second mortgage that pairs with an NHHFA first mortgage. The DPA is up to 4% of the loan amount, structured as a forgivable second lien with a four-year term. Stay in the home as your primary residence for four years and the second lien is forgiven. Sell or move out earlier and a prorated balance becomes due at sale.
Critically, this is not a loan you make payments on during those four years. There is no monthly payment on the DPA second. It sits as a silent lien, forgiving over time.
Eligibility, plain English
You qualify if all of the following are true:

items: You meet NHHFA's first-time buyer definition, or you are buying in a designated targeted area where the first-time buyer requirement is waived.
The property is in New Hampshire, owner-occupied, and either a single-family, an approved condo, or a 2-to-4-unit property where you live in one unit.
Your household income is at or below NHHFA's published limits for your county and household size. Limits vary by county; verify the current table at nhhousing.org.
Your loan is originated through an NHHFA-approved lender. LMP is approved, and so are several of our wholesale partners.
You complete an NHHFA-approved homebuyer education course before closing.

body.text: How forgiveness mechanically works

The DPA second lien is structured with a 0% interest rate and no monthly payment. The principal balance reduces by 25% each year you remain in the home as your primary residence. After four years, the lien is fully forgiven and released. Sell or refinance with cash-out at year two and 50% of the original DPA amount becomes payable at closing.

The four-year forgiveness ramp is the trade. Buyers who plan to stay at least four years pocket the full 4% as a real gift. Buyers who plan to flip or relocate within two years should run the math carefully because the prorated payback can erase the benefit.
Stacking with FHA or Conventional

Home Flex Plus pairs with NHHFA's first mortgage product, which can be either FHA, VA, USDA, or Conventional under the hood depending on your file. The DPA percentage is calculated against the first mortgage loan amount, so the dollar value of the assistance scales with the price of the house.

On a $375,000 single-family in Manchester with 3.5% FHA down ($13,125) and Home Flex Plus at 4% ($14,481 on the loan amount), the borrower can effectively close with a small surplus that often covers prepaid items and reduces cash to close to under $2,000. Without Home Flex Plus, that same buyer needs $13,125 in cash plus $5,000 to $8,000 in closing costs and prepaids. The program is the difference between buying this year or saving for two more.
The Manchester, Salem, Concord cases

We close Home Flex Plus files across New Hampshire weekly. A common case: a Manchester nurse making $65,000 buys a $325,000 condo using FHA + Home Flex Plus. Cash to close after seller credit: roughly $1,800. A Salem teacher couple at 110% AMI (above the strict NHHFA Home Preferred limit but inside Home Flex Plus tiers) buys a $425,000 single-family. The 4% DPA shifts the math from no to yes.

When Home Flex Plus is not the answer: very high-income earners above the income limits, second-home or investment buyers, and borrowers planning to move within two years. Those buyers we route to Conventional 97 + standard underwriting or to NHHFA's Home Preferred Plus if they qualify there instead.
How we run it

Fifteen minutes on a discovery call. We confirm your AMI tier against the current NHHFA county table, run preapproval on Home Flex Plus alongside FHA and Conventional 97 so you see all three side by side, and tell you which one wins for your specific file. You pick.

title: CHFA Time To Own: Connecticut's $50,000 Forgivable DPA
excerpt: Connecticut Housing Finance Authority's Time To Own program is one of the strongest down payment assistance offerings in the Northeast. Up to $50,000, fully forgivable over a 10-year ramp, available statewide for qualifying first-time buyers.

headerImage.alt: Antique brass colonial pineapple door knocker on a deep navy painted door, autumn maple leaf shadows.

flag: [DEMO COPY — pending client review · COMPLIANCE-REVIEW-PENDING · FIGURES-CURRENT-AS-OF-2026-Q1 · VERIFY-WITH-AGENCY-WEBSITE for current CHFA Time To Own program limits and forgiveness terms]

body.text: If you are buying a first home in Connecticut and you have not heard of CHFA Time To Own, that is the first thing we fix. The program is one of the most generous DPA offerings in the Northeast: up to $50,000 in down payment and closing cost assistance, structured as a forgivable second mortgage. Stay in the home for the full forgiveness ramp and the entire amount converts to a gift.
Hartford, New Haven, New London, Bridgeport, Stamford. Buyers across Connecticut use Time To Own every week. Here is how it actually works.
What Time To Own actually is

Time To Own is a CHFA-administered second mortgage that pairs with a CHFA first mortgage. Eligible borrowers can receive up to $50,000 (capped at 20% of the purchase price for some borrower categories, with higher caps for certain priority groups). The DPA can be applied to down payment, closing costs, or a combination of both.
The second mortgage carries a 0% interest rate and no monthly payment. The balance forgives over a 10-year ramp. Sell or move out before year ten and a prorated portion becomes payable at sale.
Eligibility, plain English
You qualify if all of the following are true:

items: First-time buyer (no ownership interest in a primary residence in the past three years), or buying in a CHFA-designated targeted area where the first-time buyer requirement is waived.
Property is in Connecticut, owner-occupied, and either a single-family, condo, or 2-to-4-unit where you live in one unit.
Household income is at or below CHFA's published limits for your county and household size. Limits vary across Connecticut counties; verify the current table at chfa.org.
Purchase price is at or below CHFA's purchase price limits for the property type and location.
Loan is originated through a CHFA-approved Participating Lender. LMP is approved, as are most of our wholesale partners.
You complete a CHFA-approved homebuyer education course before closing (typically 8 hours, online or in-person).

body.text: Forgiveness mechanics

The Time To Own second lien forgives at 10% per year over 10 years. After year ten, the lien is fully forgiven and released. Sell at year five and 50% of the original DPA amount becomes payable from sale proceeds. Refinance with cash-out triggers similar prorated payback rules.

The 10-year ramp is longer than New Hampshire's Home Flex Plus, which means a larger commitment to the home in exchange for a larger DPA dollar amount. That trade fits Connecticut's higher purchase prices and the typical first-time buyer's hold period.
How it stacks with the CHFA first mortgage

Time To Own pairs with a CHFA first mortgage, which can be FHA, VA, USDA, or Conventional. CHFA first-mortgage rates are typically competitive with market wholesale rates and sometimes below them, particularly for borrowers under the income limits.

On a $375,000 condo in Hartford with 3.5% FHA down ($13,125) and Time To Own covering both the down payment and most closing costs, a qualifying buyer can close with under $1,000 of their own cash. Without Time To Own, that same buyer needs $13,125 down plus $7,000 to $10,000 in closing costs. The program is the entire reason a working-class first-time buyer can afford Hartford or New Haven on today's prices.
Time To Own vs DAP

CHFA's other DPA, the Downpayment Assistance Program (DAP), is a smaller second mortgage that does carry monthly payments and is repayable, not forgivable. DAP is useful for higher-income borrowers who do not qualify for Time To Own but still want DPA. For most first-time buyers under the income limits, Time To Own wins because the forgiveness is real money.
We run both at preapproval so you see which one your file qualifies for and which one wins on the math.
When Time To Own is not the answer

Higher-income earners above CHFA limits, second-home or investment buyers, and borrowers planning to sell within three to five years should look at standard CHFA financing without Time To Own, or at non-CHFA Conventional 97 with PMI. The 10-year forgiveness ramp does not pay off if you do not stay long enough for meaningful forgiveness to accrue.
How we run it

Fifteen minutes on a discovery call. We confirm your AMI tier and county against the current CHFA table, run preapproval on Time To Own + CHFA first alongside non-CHFA Conventional 97 and FHA, and show you the cash-to-close and monthly-payment math on each. You pick the path that gets you the home you actually want.

title: FL Hometown Heroes: $35,000 for Florida's Essential Workers
excerpt: Florida's Hometown Heroes program delivers up to $35,000 in down payment and closing cost assistance to teachers, nurses, EMS, police, firefighters, and other essential workers. Available statewide and stackable with strong FHA or Conventional first mortgages.

headerImage.alt: Single weathered wooden beach chair on a quiet Florida beach at golden hour, palm tree leaning into frame, calm shoreline.

flag: [DEMO COPY — pending client review · COMPLIANCE-REVIEW-PENDING · FIGURES-CURRENT-AS-OF-2026-Q1 · VERIFY-WITH-AGENCY-WEBSITE for current FL Hometown Heroes income limits and eligible occupations list]

body.text: If you are an essential worker in Florida and you have not run the Hometown Heroes math, you are leaving money on the table. The program offers up to $35,000 in down payment and closing cost assistance to qualifying frontline workers across the state. Tampa, Cape Coral, Naples, Miami, Orlando, Jacksonville. We run this program weekly, and the answer is yes more often than borrowers expect.
What Hometown Heroes actually is

FL Hometown Heroes is administered by Florida Housing Finance Corporation. Eligible borrowers can receive up to 5% of the first mortgage loan amount, capped at $35,000, in DPA. The assistance is structured as a deferred 0% second mortgage that becomes due upon sale, refinance, or transfer of title. There is no monthly payment on the second.

The program pairs with a Florida Housing first mortgage (FHA, VA, USDA, or Conventional). The first mortgage typically carries a competitive rate, sometimes below market wholesale, because Florida Housing aggregates demand and prices accordingly.
Who qualifies as an essential worker
Hometown Heroes covers a broad list of essential occupations, updated periodically by Florida Housing. The list includes:

items: K-12 teachers and instructional support staff at Florida public schools
Healthcare workers including nurses, paramedics, EMTs, and licensed clinical professionals
Public safety: police officers, sheriff's deputies, firefighters, correctional officers
Active-duty military and veterans
Childcare workers at licensed Florida facilities
Other categories added under periodic legislative updates (verify the current eligibility list at floridahousing.org)

body.text: If you work full-time in one of these roles for a Florida employer, you likely qualify on the occupation side. The income side is where most files get tested.
Income, property, and credit eligibility
You qualify if all of the following are true:

items: You work full-time in an eligible Florida-based essential occupation.
Your household income is at or below 150% of the county Area Median Income (AMI), which is one of the more generous limits in the country, particularly in higher-cost Florida counties.
The property is in Florida, owner-occupied, and a single-family, condo, townhome, or eligible 2-to-4-unit.
Loan is originated through a Florida Housing-approved Participating Lender. LMP is approved.
You complete a HUD-approved homebuyer education course before closing.
FICO score and DTI meet the underlying first-mortgage program guidelines (FHA, VA, etc.).

body.text: How it stacks with FL Assist and Salute Our Soldiers

Florida Housing offers two other major DPA programs: FL Assist (a smaller flat-dollar DPA available to broader eligibility) and Salute Our Soldiers (specifically for veterans and active military). These programs do not stack with Hometown Heroes; you pick one.

For most essential workers, Hometown Heroes wins because the dollar amount is larger and the income limits are more generous. Veterans should run both Hometown Heroes and Salute Our Soldiers because Salute often pairs with stronger first-mortgage pricing for VA-eligible borrowers.
The Tampa, Cape Coral, Naples, Miami cases

A Tampa public school teacher making $58,000 buys a $295,000 townhome using FHA + Hometown Heroes. Cash to close after seller credit: under $2,500. Without Hometown Heroes, that same buyer needs roughly $13,000 in cash. The program is the difference between buying this year or saving for three more in a market that is moving against them.

A Cape Coral firefighter and a Naples nurse, married, combined income $135,000, buy a $425,000 single-family. They are well under 150% AMI for Lee and Collier counties combined. Hometown Heroes covers most of the down payment and closing costs. They close with cash reserves intact, which matters when you are buying in hurricane-coverage country.
How we run it

Fifteen minutes on a discovery call. We confirm your occupation eligibility, run your income against the current AMI table for your Florida county, and pre-approve you on Hometown Heroes + the right first-mortgage program. You see the cash-to-close and the monthly payment before we get further into your file. If Hometown Heroes wins, we move. If a different path wins, we move on that instead.

title: Stacking CHFA-CO with metroDPA: How Denver-Metro Buyers Combine Programs
excerpt: Colorado Housing and Finance Authority and Denver-metro's metroDPA are two separate down payment assistance programs that can stack legally for qualifying buyers in Denver, Aurora, and the surrounding metro counties. Here is how the math actually works.

headerImage.alt: Single weathered wooden birdhouse on a tall cedar post against Rocky Mountain Front Range silhouette at dusk, prairie grass foreground.

flag: [DEMO COPY — pending client review · COMPLIANCE-REVIEW-PENDING · FIGURES-CURRENT-AS-OF-2026-Q1 · VERIFY-WITH-AGENCY-WEBSITE for current CHFA-CO program limits, metroDPA forgiveness terms, and stacking eligibility]

body.text: Colorado has one of the more interesting DPA stacking opportunities in the country. CHFA, the state housing finance authority, runs its statewide programs. Denver-metro counties operate their own separate metroDPA program. Qualifying buyers in the Denver metro can combine the two, layering DPA on top of DPA, and walk into a home with a fraction of the cash they thought they needed.
Here is how the stacking actually works, when it is legal, and where the math lands in Front Range, Aurora, Boulder, and Colorado Springs.
What CHFA-CO offers
Colorado Housing and Finance Authority runs several first-mortgage and DPA flavors:

items: CHFA FirstStep: 30-year fixed FHA first mortgage with paired DPA, designed for first-time buyers under income limits.
CHFA FirstGeneration: targeted at first-generation homebuyers with enhanced DPA (verify current dollar amount at chfainfo.com).
CHFA SmartStep: Conventional first-mortgage option for moderate-income buyers, with DPA structured as a second-mortgage forgivable lien.
CHFA Preferred: standard Conventional with smaller DPA, broader eligibility.

body.text: Each flavor pairs with a CHFA DPA second mortgage of varying size, typically structured as either a forgivable grant (smaller dollar, full forgiveness) or a repayable second (larger dollar, monthly payment).
What metroDPA is

metroDPA is administered by the City and County of Denver and is available across most Denver-metro counties (Adams, Arapahoe, Boulder, Broomfield, Denver, Douglas, Jefferson). Eligible buyers can receive up to 6% of the first mortgage loan amount as a fully forgivable second lien, with the full balance forgiving after a defined occupancy period (typically 3 years; verify current terms at metrodpa.org).
metroDPA is not a CHFA product. It is a separate program with its own eligibility rules, income limits, and Participating Lender list. LMP is approved on metroDPA.
When the two programs can stack
Stacking is allowed in specific scenarios where the underlying first-mortgage product permits subordinate DPA from multiple sources. Generally:

items: metroDPA can layer on top of certain CHFA first-mortgage products as a third-position lien (CHFA first, CHFA DPA second, metroDPA third).
Stacking requires both programs' eligibility tests to be passed independently. Income limits, residency, first-time buyer status, and approved-lender requirements must all be met.

Some CHFA products do not permit additional subordinate financing and cannot be stacked with metroDPA. We verify the specific CHFA flavor against metroDPA stacking permissions on every file before we promise the stack.

body.text: Stacking is not automatic and not universal. Run by an LO who knows the rules, not by a borrower reading two agency websites and assuming.
The stacking math

Hypothetical Denver-metro buyer: $425,000 home, FHA first mortgage at 3.5% down ($14,875). CHFA FirstStep DPA covers the down payment via a forgivable grant of up to 4% ($16,400 against the loan amount). metroDPA layers on top with up to 6% additional ($24,600). Total DPA stacked: roughly $41,000 against $425,000 purchase. Cash to close after seller credit: typically under $2,000 for a qualifying file.

Without the stack, that same buyer needs $14,875 down plus $8,000 to $11,000 in closing costs. The stack is the entire reason a moderate-income Denver-metro buyer can compete in a Front Range market that has priced out half the local workforce.
When stacking does not make sense

Three buyers should not stack. Higher-income borrowers above metroDPA limits cannot use metroDPA at all, so the stack is unavailable. Buyers planning to sell within three years lose most of the metroDPA forgiveness benefit and may face prorated payback. And buyers in non-metro Colorado counties (Colorado Springs, Pueblo, Grand Junction, mountain communities) cannot use metroDPA because the geographic eligibility is metro-only; for those buyers, CHFA standalone is the right path.
How we run it

Fifteen minutes on a discovery call. We confirm your county against metroDPA's geographic boundary, run your income against both CHFA and metroDPA limits, and identify the specific CHFA flavor that permits the metroDPA stack on top. Then we run the stacked preapproval alongside the standalone-CHFA preapproval and standalone Conventional 97 so you see all three side by side. You pick the path that gets you the home you actually want with the lowest cash to close that fits your file.
